modules.module: M1: Why Most Side Projects Die and How Agile Fixes That

Claude.notes: Strong opening narrative with Marcus and the Napster reference; concept emerges clearly from the failure pattern. Four lessons in a single module makes pacing dense — the 'foundational module' carries a lot of weight that later modules then partially repeat.
Claude.topIssue: Module 1 packs four lessons (Agile basics, backlog, sprint mechanics, feedback loops) that overlap meaningfully with M3 and M6. The density risks a learner reading L1+L2 then deferring L3+L4 indefinitely.

Gemini.notes: Agile Manifesto framing (2001, Utah, 17 signers, Waterfall reaction) and definitions of sprint/backlog/velocity are technically accurate. The Shawn Fanning detail is the weak point: Fanning was 18 when he wrote Napster in 1998–99 and 19 at launch, not 22. The claim that AI tools have compressed CRUD-app build time from a weekend to '45 minutes of prompting' is also overstated for production-quality work.
Gemini.topIssue: Shawn Fanning was 18, not 22, when he created Napster — a verifiable factual error in the opening narrative.

Perplexity.notes: Currency is excellent: Cursor, Lovable, Bolt.new, Replit Agent are all 2024–2025 representative tools. The Marcus story is dated to March 2024, which keeps it concrete. The Napster framing is dated by design but the age detail is wrong.
Perplexity.topIssue: Real-world tooling references are current, but the historical Napster anecdote anchors a key claim on a misremembered fact (Fanning's age).

Claude.notes: The Vague Idea Graveyard / Customer Who Actually Exists / Five Whys / First Transaction sequence builds well. Marcus and Priya both reappear here as completely different students (different schools, majors, ideas) than they were in M1 — same first names, different identities. That's the biggest narrative liability.
Claude.topIssue: Character recycling: Marcus is now a UC San Diego cog-sci junior building an AI study app, but in M1 he was a Georgia Tech CS+econ junior building an internship-ghosting Chrome extension. Same name, different person — confusing for a learner reading sequentially.

Gemini.notes: The 'three flavors of vague' (too broad / too shallow / too hypothetical) is a useful original taxonomy. The 4–6 week buildability test and 'demographic vs customer' distinction are technically sound. Coverage of how to use AI to constrain rather than validate is correctly framed.
Gemini.topIssue: Some original frameworks (Three Flavors of Vague) are course-coined; learners shouldn't expect to encounter the exact terminology elsewhere — minor risk of treating it as canonical.

Perplexity.notes: Devon's first-gen-college-students financial-aid-appeals example reads as plausibly current and the Notion-template-business sibling business is well-calibrated to 2024–2025 indie hacker reality. The lesson on AI as a yes-machine when asked 'is this a good idea' aligns with current Claude/ChatGPT behavior.
Perplexity.topIssue: Examples are current but the recurring-character-across-modules pattern doesn't carry continuity — each Marcus/Priya feels like a fresh draft rather than the same protagonist learning across the course.

modules.module: M3: Sprint Cycles for Solo Builders

Claude.notes: Sprint mechanics for solo builders are the genuine strength here — the 'commitment device against your own cognitive drift' framing is sharp and the Sunday-night/parking-lot/daily-micro-check minimum system is concrete. Sprint goal vs sprint backlog vs sprint review key terms are well-bounded.
Claude.topIssue: Marcus appears yet again — this time as an Austin dorm-room builder with an AI resume scanner. The AI study-resume-extension recycling across M1/M3/M4 starts to feel like name reuse rather than character development.

Gemini.notes: Sprint goal as observable outcome ('users can sign up and see a dashboard') vs activity ('work on auth') is a clean distillation of standard agile practice. Recommendation of one-week sprints over two-week as a calibration choice for solo builders is defensible.
Gemini.topIssue: Module 3 covers ground that overlaps with M1 lesson 3 (running a sprint). A learner taking both will encounter the Sunday-night ritual twice without the second framing acknowledging the first.

Perplexity.notes: Examples remain current. The 80-hours-no-demo failure mode is recognizable as a 2024–2025 phenomenon for AI-assisted builders. No tool dependency that would date the lab.
Perplexity.topIssue: Recommends agile concepts that map cleanly to current solo-builder practice, but doesn't reference any 2024–2025-specific developments (Linear's solo plan, GitHub Projects, etc.) that learners might actually use.

modules.module: M4: AI Tools for Every Stage

Claude.notes: Strongest module of the course on applied outcome. The four-research-jobs taxonomy (landscape mapping / problem synthesis / interview design / assumption mapping) gives the AI-tool conversation a structure most courses lack. Lab is highly executable.
Claude.topIssue: Marcus (yet another version) reappears here as a Georgia Tech CS junior with a Chrome extension that tracked study hours — different from the Marcus in M1 with the same school and major.

Gemini.notes: Tool-to-job mapping (Perplexity for landscape, Reddit-via-AI for problem synthesis, Claude/ChatGPT for interview design and assumption mapping) is technically appropriate. The explicit limits section ('AI synthesizes existing public knowledge — it doesn't generate new signal') is one of the most accurate framings in the course.
Gemini.topIssue: The behavioral-vs-hypothetical distinction in customer discovery ('walk me through the last time' beats 'would you use') is taught well, but Rob Fitzpatrick's The Mom Test is the canonical source and is not credited here (he is cited later in M6).

Perplexity.notes: Most current module of the course. Names Perplexity, Claude, ChatGPT (with browsing), GPT-4o, You.com — all 2024–2025-relevant. The steelman-the-competitor and assumption-mapping prompts are the kind learners can actually run today and get useful output.
Perplexity.topIssue: Strong currency, but explicitly low-online-footprint communities (tradespeople, certain immigrant communities) get one paragraph — a learner who needs that path will want a longer follow-up.

modules.module: M5: Handling Scope Creep When AI Makes Everything Feel Possible

Claude.notes: Maya is a fresh character who doesn't collide with prior modules. The 'Yes Machine' framing of LLM helpfulness as a structural bias toward expansion is one of the sharpest pedagogical moves in the course — it names a problem that learners are absolutely encountering right now.
Claude.topIssue: The four creep types (feature/audience/platform/quality) are introduced as a list rather than dramatized — the lesson is the strongest in the course conceptually but the dramatization weakens slightly in section 'Three Types of Scope Creep' (which actually lists four).

Gemini.notes: Definition of Done (specific user action + specific user + binary test) is correctly scoped — this is closer to a lightweight DoD than the Scrum-canonical team-level DoD, which is a defensible adaptation. The MVB (Minimum Viable Boundary) is course-coined and may confuse learners encountering MVP literature.
Gemini.topIssue: Section heading 'Three Types of Scope Creep AI Accelerates' actually contains four types (feature/audience/platform/quality). Minor copy issue but inconsistent.

Perplexity.notes: The Yes Machine framing is exactly the failure mode current LLMs exhibit — Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini all default toward additive responses to brainstorming prompts. Maya's three-hour scope explosion is a recognizable 2024–2025 pattern.
Perplexity.topIssue: Highly current pedagogy. Could be even sharper by referencing the constitutional AI / RLHF training dynamics that produce the Yes Machine pattern, but that may be appropriate Module 6+ material.

Claude.notes: The 340-signups-to-11-returns gap is a sharp, memorable framing of the politeness loop. Three-types-of-dangerous-positive-feedback (compliments without commitments / future-tense endorsements / feature requests as praise) is the strongest concrete diagnostic in the course.
Claude.topIssue: Marcus appears yet again. By module 6 the recycling has crossed from minor liability to active confusion — five different Marcuses in six modules, all college students, all building different AI projects.

Gemini.notes: Rob Fitzpatrick attribution for 'compliment-shaped noise' is approximately correct — Fitzpatrick's The Mom Test (2013) is the source for the broader thesis though that exact phrase is paraphrased, not a direct quote. Behavioral-validation taxonomy (return / referral / complaint / payment) is well-bounded.
Gemini.topIssue: 'Compliment-shaped noise' is attributed to Rob Fitzpatrick but doesn't appear as a verbatim phrase in The Mom Test — learners googling the term may not find the source.

Perplexity.notes: Cold outreach playbook (Module 6 lesson 2) maps to current Discord / subreddit / DM realities. Pivot decision framework in lesson 4 reads as current.
Perplexity.topIssue: The Mom Test is from 2013 but remains the canonical reference; tapping it is appropriate. No staleness issues spotted.

modules.module: M7: Turning a Side Project into a Portfolio Piece or Income Stream

Claude.notes: Priya's interview-stumble scene is the cleanest narrative-creates-problem moment in the course — the 'AI did most of the work' line is a real failure mode. Decision log + AI contribution statement framework is genuinely useful applied content.
Claude.topIssue: Priya the M7 Chrome-extension-builder is a different person than Priya the M1/M2 meal-planning / dining-hall student. Recurring first names without continuity hurts character integrity.

Gemini.notes: Five-component documentation set (problem statement / architecture / decision log / limitations / AI contribution) is technically sound and matches what hiring managers actually look for. Use-AI-as-interviewer rather than ghostwriter is the correct division of labor.
Gemini.topIssue: Pricing Strategy lesson (lesson 3) is referenced but not deeply scrutinized in this review pass; technical pricing frameworks (cost-plus / value-based / competitive) likely covered but should be verified.

Perplexity.notes: AI contribution statements as a 2025 hiring norm is exactly current — major firms (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and many smaller employers now expect explicit acknowledgement. The 'I used Claude to scaffold but rewrote the API integration because of rate-limiting' example reads as real and current.
Perplexity.topIssue: Most current module on hiring/portfolio practice. Scaling-and-ethics lesson (lesson 4) could be grounded in 2024–2025 specific examples (Wispr Flow, Cursor, etc.) for sharper currency.

Claude.notes: Strongest capstone in the course. Maya's three-tabs-at-midnight opening lands the option-paralysis problem cleanly. Four-gate viability filter + Week 3 Test + MLP definition give learners a concrete decision framework before committing 30 days.
Claude.topIssue: Module 8 carries the heaviest applied weight (a real 30-day commitment), but the connection back to M5 Maya isn't drawn — a small continuity nod would strengthen the course arc.

Gemini.notes: Most of the framework is sound. The MLP/MVP distinction ('MVP = minimum viable, MLP = minimum I'd let a stranger use') is course-coined; in the broader literature MVP already implies a usable version, and the Reis MVP definition (Lean Startup, 2011) does not require something embarrassingly broken. Introducing MLP as a competing term may confuse learners reading external Lean Startup material.
Gemini.topIssue: MLP terminology is course-invented and competes with the established MVP definition rather than refining it; learners will encounter MVP everywhere else and may not understand why this course uses different terminology.

Perplexity.notes: 30-day sprint framing is well-calibrated to current indie hacker reality (Pieter Levels, Ben Stokes, etc.). Day 8 / Day 18 / Day 28 checkpoint logic reads as current and applicable today. Lab is fully executable in-academy via the AI sprint advisor.
Perplexity.topIssue: Could reference current 30-day-build communities (e.g. Buildspace, Replit's launch competitions) for a learner who wants peer accountability outside this course; otherwise the framework holds up well as-is.
